original_abstract: The two-stage time series design has emerged as a cornerstone of analytical methodology in environmental epidemiology. Recent advancements have significantly enhanced both stages of this framework: distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs) now allow for the simultaneous exploration of non-linear effects and delayed temporal dependencies, while multivariate meta-analysis provides a robust mechanism for pooling complex, multi-parameter associations across different locations. Despite these methodological breakthroughs, the integrated application of these two techniques remains infrequent and lacks a standardized procedural approach.
humanized_abstract: The two-stage time series design has been widely adopted in environmental epidemiology as a backbone analytical strategy. Recent advances in the two components of the two-stage design have greatly empowered the capacity of each stage of the design. Distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs) are capable of offering insights into non-linear relationships between exposure and outcome and exploring temporal lag impacts in a highly flexible and informative manner. Meanwhile, multivariate meta-analysis has been established as a statistically sound framework to synthesize high dimensional parameter estimates from individual studies. The combined use of these recent methodologies has not yet become a commonly practice, and a standardized methodology for carrying out the synthesis at the second stage of the two-stage time series design is missing.

The combined use of these recent methodologies has not yet become a commonly practice, and a standardized methodology for carrying out the synthesis at the second stage of the two-stage time series design is missing.
